museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: The Atari landfill becomes a symbol, computer games keep thriving, and the UK micro scene feels busy, local and strangely insulated from the worst American console damage.
museum.curatorNote: The Alamogordo burial is one of 1983's most mythologised stories. This exhibit treats it as a verified disposal event that later became larger than itself in memory.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: September is where business failure becomes folklore.

items.title: Atari cartridges are buried in Alamogordo
items.body: Atari disposed of unsold and returned stock in a New Mexico landfill, an event later inflated into shorthand for the crash itself.
items.sourceNote: Atari video game burial sources place disposal in September 1983.

items.title: The crash hardens into a public story
items.body: By autumn, the US console downturn had become impossible to frame as a temporary wobble.
items.sourceNote: Crash histories describe 1983 revenue collapse and publisher failures.

items.title: Home-computer games keep gaining cultural weight
items.body: Lode Runner, Ultima III, EA's first wave and UK Spectrum hits made the computer side feel creative even as console retail looked damaged.

items.title: UK players experience a different recession of faith
items.body: The American crash mattered, but British high streets were also full of microcomputer promise, budget tapes and magazine enthusiasm.

items.title: Atari's symbol changes
items.body: Atari had once meant the future arriving early. By September, it could also mean overreach, waste and a market that had misread itself.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight games and objects in September's shadow.

items.title: Atari landfill stock
items.body: Not a release, but an anti-release: cartridges leaving the market as waste rather than play.

items.title: Lode Runner
items.body: Still fresh as a puzzle-platformer with editability built into its identity.

items.title: Ultima III: Exodus
items.body: A newly released RPG showing the scale computers could hold.

items.title: Manic Miner
items.body: A UK platformer whose rooms already felt like folk memory.

items.title: Jetpac
items.body: Still a benchmark for fast, compact Spectrum design.

items.title: Dragon's Lair
items.body: The laserdisc cabinet remained a talking point, admired and argued about.

items.title: Star Wars
items.body: Atari's vector cabinet still offered a cleaner symbol of Atari craft than its home-console business did.

items.title: Ant Attack build-up
items.body: Quicksilva's isometric survival game belongs to the late-1983 Spectrum story, with exact month often treated broadly.
items.sourceNote: Ant Attack references list 1983; exact month not asserted.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: September's case is about what machines leave behind.

items.title: Atari 2600 surplus
items.body: The same installed base that once guaranteed power now made the glut physically visible.

items.title: ZX Spectrum
items.body: The Spectrum's cassette market gave Britain a different relationship to 1983's downturn.

items.title: Apple II and C64
items.body: Computer-game platforms carried much of the year's creative prestige.
items.sourceNote: Lode Runner, Ultima III and EA context.

items.title: Arcade vector cabinets
items.body: Star Wars showed Atari could still make elegant arcade hardware while its home business struggled.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: September magazines had to explain a culture splitting apart.

items.title: Electronic Games
items.headline: The crash becomes visible
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Electronic Games cover for September 1983.
items.curatorNote: The American magazine shelf could no longer treat the downturn as background noise.
items.body: The American magazine shelf could no longer treat the downturn as background noise.

items.title: Computer and Video Games
items.headline: UK micro energy against US console gloom
items.curatorNote: C&VG's mixed remit made it well suited to a fractured year.
items.body: C&VG's mixed remit made it well suited to a fractured year.

items.title: Your Computer
items.headline: Computer ownership after the console wobble
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Your Computer cover for September 1983.
items.curatorNote: A serious-computing frame made games feel safer from the crash narrative.
items.body: A serious-computing frame made games feel safer from the crash narrative.

items.title: ZX Computing
items.headline: Spectrum software keeps moving
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed ZX Computing cover for September 1983.
items.curatorNote: The Spectrum scene did not feel like a market ending; it felt like a queue forming.
items.body: The Spectrum scene did not feel like a market ending; it felt like a queue forming.

items.title: The landfill story travelled well
items.body: It was simple, visual and perfect for retelling.

items.title: Local scenes contradicted headlines
items.body: A UK player with a Spectrum might not feel the industry was dying at all.

items.title: Reviews replaced trust
items.body: After too many weak cartridges, buyers needed stronger filters.

items.title: Computer clubs kept optimism alive
items.body: Seeing software shared in person made the future feel active.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: September felt like a split between shame and invention.

items.title: Atari became a cautionary object
items.body: The landfill turned business failure into something almost archaeological.

items.title: Computer games felt alive
items.body: The best home-computer releases made the crash feel incomplete as an explanation.

items.title: Britain felt adjacent, not untouched
items.body: The news mattered, but the local shelf told another story.

items.title: Memory started simplifying early
items.body: Even as it happened, the crash wanted a symbol.

sources.label: Atari video game burial
sources.note: Used for September 1983 Alamogordo disposal and mythology caveat.

sources.label: Ant Attack context
sources.note: Used for 1983 Spectrum release context; exact month not asserted.
